auth.have_account: Already have an account? Sign In
auth.login_error: Login failed. Please check your credentials.
auth.register_error: Registration failed. Try a different username.
auth.register_success: Registration successful! You can now log in.
auth.username_space_warning: Spaces are not allowed in usernames
auth.error_username_taken: This username is already taken. Please choose another one.
auth.register_error_generic: Something went wrong. Please try again later.
auth.help_rule_explain_text: A simple budget formula: 50% of income for Needs (housing, food), 30% for Wants (hobbies, entertainment), 20% for Savings. The button above automatically sets your savings goal based on your salary.
auth.disclaimer_short: By registering, you agree to our Terms and
auth.disclaimer_full: This service is currently in beta testing. To ensure system stability and optimize our AI algorithms, the development team may perform technical diagnostics and data processing within the platform. We strictly commit to never sharing your user data with third parties.

dashboard.title: Financial Overview
dashboard.welcome_body: Your finances are under control. Find detailed analytics by category in the Statistics section.
dashboard.tama_no_facts: No facts yet today — check back later!
dashboard.ufo_intro: Welcome back! In these sections, you will find useful functionality
dashboard.ufo_cat_desc: Here you can create your spending categories
dashboard.ufo_tx_desc: Here you can add and sort your transactions
dashboard.ufo_stats_desc: Here you can see your statistics for any period
dashboard.weekly_no_budget: Set a monthly budget to track weekly spending
dashboard.weekly_no_cycle: Set your payday to enable weekly tracking
dashboard.budget_insight_reason_up: You're under today's budget target. If you keep this pace, your next weekly allowance will be higher.
dashboard.budget_insight_reason_down: You've exceeded today's budget target. Your next weekly allowance will be adjusted down.
dashboard.budget_insight_cheer_up: Great job saving! Your limit for next week will be increased because of this discipline.
dashboard.budget_insight_cheer_down: Your limit for next week will decrease. Stay disciplined for a better week ahead!
dashboard.tama_hint_star: Find and click the colored star if you want an interesting fact!
dashboard.tama_journal_empty: You haven't caught any cosmic secrets today! Watch the stars or look out for floating cows!

categories.title: Manage Categories
categories.error_load: Failed to load categories. Check server connection.
categories.error_delete: Failed to delete. Category may be in use.

statistics.title: Analytics
statistics.forecast_positive: Projected surplus by end of month
statistics.forecast_negative: Projected deficit by end of month
statistics.forecast_modal_context: At your current spending rate of {{rate}}/day, you will have {{balance}} {{direction}} by end of month.
statistics.forecast_modal_context_payday: At your current spending rate of {{rate}}/day, you will have {{balance}} {{direction}} by payday.
statistics.savings_forecast_tip_pos: You're on a great savings trajectory! Consider building an investment portfolio.
statistics.savings_forecast_tip_neg: Spending outpaces income this cycle. Review your top expense categories.

settings.title: Settings
settings.ai_advice_desc: Get data-driven tips based on your spending patterns
settings.ai_humor_desc: Receive fun facts and jokes when idle
settings.rule_tooltip: 50% Needs + 30% Wants = 80% spending limit. The remaining 20% goes to savings.
settings.rule_applied: Applied! Spending goal set to {{goal}} (80% of salary). Save the other 20%.
settings.delete_modal_body: This action is permanent and cannot be undone. All your transactions, categories, and settings will be deleted forever.
settings.delete_error: Failed to delete account. Please try again.

salary_just_in: Fuel loaded! The month is in motion — now let's fly straight and not do anything reckless.
Payday confirmed. The real challenge begins: keeping it together for 4 weeks.
Income received. The budget engine is live. Stick to the plan and we'll land in the green.
Money in the dock! A wise co-pilot once said: the hardest part isn't earning — it's not spending it all on day one.
Salary detected. Remember: it's not what you earn, it's what you keep. Let's keep most of it.
Fresh funds in the account. No need to celebrate with a splurge — the real celebration is a healthy balance next month.
Payday achieved! Now's a great time to review your spending plan before the month gets away from you.
Income confirmed. I'll be watching the weekly pace — let's see if this month beats the last.
The tank is full. Let's see how far we can go before the next refuel. Smooth spending ahead.
Salary landed. Breathe. There's no rush to spend it — it'll be there when you actually need it.
Funds received! Quick reminder: a budget isn't a punishment. It's your flight plan for the month.
Good news: income detected. Better news: you still remember to track it. You're already ahead.
Your monthly fuel arrived. The financial flight plan is active. Ready when you are.
Payday! Exciting, right? Now comes the strategic part — where does it actually go?
Income recorded. No spending anxiety needed — just awareness. That's what we're here for.
Salary in the books. The clock has officially started on this month's budget run.
Funds detected, co-pilot engaged! If you set a spending goal, this is the moment it becomes real.
Your income arrived safely. I'll be watching the weekly pace so you don't have to stress about it.
Payday confirmed. Pro tip: the first 10 minutes after payday are the most important for your budget.
Money received and logged. No need to do anything dramatic — just don't do anything dramatic.
pacing_over: Weekly budget exceeded by {{percent}}%. We've entered turbulence — time to reduce altitude.
Houston, we have a spending situation. {{percent}}% over the weekly limit. Time to recalibrate.
The weekly pacing light is red. {{percent}}% over limit. Can we coast for the rest of the week?
Weekly limit: breached by {{percent}}%. Not ideal, but recoverable. Just no more big moves this week.
Over by {{percent}}% this week. The month is still saveable — it just needs a quieter finish.
Weekly pace: {{percent}}% over. Even good pilots hit turbulence. Let's stabilize.
Spending this week outpaced the plan by {{percent}}%. The radar is red — hold the controls steady.
We're {{percent}}% over the weekly norm. Nothing catastrophic — but let's not make it worse.
Weekly limit has been exceeded by {{percent}}%. Budget shield took a hit. Switching to repair mode.
Oof. {{percent}}% over the weekly goal. Not a disaster — but worth a 'no extra spending' rest of week.
Weekly budget: {{percent}}% exceeded. Happens to the best plans. Just don't let it compound.
Heads up: weekly pacing is {{percent}}% over target. The monthly goal is still reachable with a lighter touch.
Red alert: {{percent}}% over weekly limit. Friendly advice — no impulse buys until Monday.
Weekly tracker shows {{percent}}% overspend. Consider this your friendly speed camera.
You've gone {{percent}}% over the weekly limit. The month isn't lost — just recalibrate now.
Weekly overrun: {{percent}}%. Small turbulence. Stay the course and we'll land smoothly anyway.
{{percent}}% over the weekly plan — but you're aware of it, which is already 80% of the solution.
A {{percent}}% weekly overrun isn't fun, but it's fixable. Lighter spending for 3 days fixes most of it.
Weekly spending is running {{percent}}% hot. Time to cool the engines until the next week.
The weekly pace hit {{percent}}% over target. This is a checkpoint, not a crisis. Keep tracking.
pacing_warn: Getting close to the weekly ceiling. Maybe hold off on non-essentials for a day or two?
We're approaching the weekly limit. Time to switch to economy mode until the weekend.
Budget radar: approaching threshold. Not there yet — but worth a gentle brake.
Pacing is a bit fast. Nothing wrong — just keep an eye on the next couple of days.
You've used most of this week's budget. Coasting to Sunday might be the right call.
Weekly pace: orange zone. Not red yet — just staying watchful.
Almost at the weekly limit. Maybe a good week to rediscover free entertainment?
The spending meter is approaching the weekly mark. A quiet finish to the week would balance things out.
Getting close to the limit for this week. The good news: you noticed. The plan is working.
Heads up — weekly spending is in the caution range. A mindful day or two and you're back in the clear.
A bit above the midpoint for the weekly limit. Worth pausing before the next purchase.
We're in the yellow zone for weekly pacing. Perfectly fixable with a calm end to the week.
Budget pacing light: amber. Not alarming, but worth noticing before it turns red.
Weekly limit is within sight. Time to shift gears and let the week coast home.
Close to the weekly budget cap. No panic — just a good time to defer anything non-urgent.
Pacing is a little fast this week. The weekend has a way of adding up — keep that in mind.
The budget is approaching the weekly ceiling. Slowing down now means more breathing room later.
We're tracking close to the weekly limit. Worth flagging before the weekend spending kicks in.
Weekly spending is elevated but manageable. Stay steady and you'll land under the monthly goal.
Approaching the weekly budget mark. One of those weeks where just browsing can be dangerous.
pacing_great: You're crushing it this week — at half the weekly pace. Treat yourself, you've earned it!
Mission control confirms: exceptional budget trajectory. No corrections needed.
At this rate, you'll have serious surplus by month-end. Save it, invest it, or splurge wisely.
Weekly spending is at half the expected pace and it's already midweek. That's genuinely impressive.
Outstanding financial discipline this week. The co-pilot is taking notes.
You're running this week at incredible efficiency. The budget loves you right now.
If every week looked like this one, you'd hit your monthly goals with room to spare. Keep it going.
Past midweek and you've only used half the weekly budget. Truly exceptional.
Your spending pace this week is excellent. You've earned a guilt-free small treat if you want one.
Financial co-pilot status update: you're doing better than 90% of people who track their budget.
Halfway through the week, less than half the budget used. You're a financial co-pilot's dream.
Weekly pace: phenomenal. You're building a buffer that'll save you when life gets expensive.
This week's spending is running beautifully lean. The monthly math is definitely in your favor.
Low spending week! Every day like this is money working for future-you.
We're looking at an exceptional week. The budget is very, very happy with your choices.
You're tracking at half-pace this week and it's already late in the week. Respect.
Great weekly pace! You've bought yourself margin for any unexpected expenses ahead.
Weekly efficiency report: excellent. You're in the zone most people only dream about.
Minimal spending, maximum awareness — that's the formula. You've got it this week.
Less than half your weekly budget used past midweek. That's not luck — that's strategy.
balanced: Spending across categories looks well-balanced this week. Even financial pilots appreciate variety.
No single category is dominating your budget. That's textbook healthy spending.
Food, transport, leisure — all in proportion. The co-pilot approves of the balance.
Your spending distribution this week is surprisingly healthy. Diverse expenses, no outliers.
Balance detected: multiple categories, no single one running away. This is what good budgeting looks like.
Spending spread across categories nicely. No alarms, no red flags — just a well-distributed week.
Healthy category mix this week. You're spending on life, not just one corner of it.
Everything is proportional this week — food, transport, leisure. That's financial maturity.
Spending balance looks clean. No single category consuming everything. Well done.
Multiple categories, balanced amounts — it's a rare and beautiful thing. Keep it up.
The budget breakdown looks healthy across the board. Nothing is obviously out of control.
No dominant spending category this week. That's a sign of intentional, balanced decision-making.
Your expenses are spread proportionally. Real life, balanced spending — exactly how it should work.
Balanced category distribution detected. No single area is eating the budget alive. Refreshing.
All spending categories are pulling their weight without overloading any one of them. Solid week.
Even your spending looks balanced — essentials, experiences, everything in check.
The weekly spending pattern is remarkably well-proportioned. No categories in crisis mode.
A nice, balanced week of spending. Nothing extreme, nothing neglected. Just sensible living.
Category balance: on point. You're not over-indexing on any single area. That's the goal.
Spending diversity is healthy this week. A mix of categories with no single outlier. Good living.
pacing_good: Weekly pace: on track. You're exactly where you need to be.
Under the weekly limit. That's not luck — that's discipline. Nice.
Budget check: healthy. Weekly spending is comfortable. Keep the rhythm.
You're tracking well this week. Nothing to flag — just keep doing what you're doing.
All good on the weekly front. The budget looks sustainable at this pace.
Weekly spending is comfortably below the limit. Room to breathe, no need to stress.
On pace, under budget. The flight is smooth at current altitude.
Weekly tracker: green. Spending is steady and sustainable. Solid work.
Nothing dramatic to report this week. Budget is healthy, pace is good — rare and wonderful.
Under the weekly spending limit. The financial forecast looks calm and manageable.
This week's spending is exactly what the plan called for. No issues, no worries.
The budget is breathing easily this week. No turbulence, good altitude.
Clean week so far. Spending is in the right range and nothing looks suspicious.
Weekly pacing: just right. Not too tight, not too loose. Textbook budget management.
You're tracking under the weekly limit. Things are looking exactly as they should.
Budget check-in: all green. The weekly pace is smooth and on track.
Everything looks in order this week. Spending is sensible and within the plan.
Weekly pace: healthy. No corrections needed. Just maintain this course.
The numbers look good this week. You're making the budget work instead of the other way around.
Under the weekly ceiling and still living your life. That's the goal — and you're hitting it.
tips: The 50/30/20 rule: spend 50% on needs, 30% on wants, save 20%. Simple, effective, powerful.
Pay yourself first — set aside savings the moment you receive income, before any other expense.
An emergency fund covering 3–6 months of expenses protects you from most financial shocks.
Automate your savings: if it's automatic, you'll never forget — and never accidentally spend it.
Review your subscriptions monthly — the average person pays for 2–3 services they've forgotten about.
The 24-hour rule: wait a full day before any unplanned purchase over your threshold. Most urges pass.
Diversify income sources. Even a small side project can protect against sudden job loss.
Credit score matters: a good score can save you thousands in interest over a lifetime.
Investing small amounts early is more powerful than investing large amounts later — time beats size.
Track every expense for one month. Awareness alone changes behavior — guaranteed.
Negotiate everything: salary, rent, insurance, internet. Most rates are starting points, not final prices.
Buy experiences over things. Research shows experiences provide more lasting happiness.
A health insurance gap can erase years of savings overnight. Never skip health coverage.
Learn one new personal finance concept each month. Knowledge compounds like interest.
Set a 'fun money' allowance — guilt-free spending within a fixed limit prevents budget fatigue.
Think net worth, not just income. Net worth = assets minus liabilities. Grow the gap.
Index funds historically outperform most actively managed funds over 10+ years.
The earlier you start a retirement account, the less you need to contribute to reach the same goal.
Lifestyle inflation is silent. When income rises, resist automatically raising spending.
Avoid financial advice from people trying to sell you something. Conflict of interest kills good advice.
Dollar-cost averaging: invest a fixed amount regularly regardless of market prices. Removes emotion.
Insurance is risk management. Protect against catastrophic loss before optimizing returns.
A written budget is 40% more effective than a mental one. Write it down.
The biggest financial risk in your 20s is not starting. In your 50s: not having started in your 20s.
Reduce high-interest debt first. Paying off a 20% APR card beats almost any investment return.
Use cash-back cards — but only if you pay the full balance monthly. Interest destroys the reward.
Housing costs should not exceed 30% of gross income. Going above creates financial fragility.
Teach children about money early. Financial habits form before age 10, research shows.
Review your insurance coverage annually — life changes mean coverage needs change too.
Small leaks sink great ships: $5/day in unnecessary spending = $1,825/year = a solid investment.
The 'poor' pay interest. The 'rich' earn interest. Focus on switching sides as fast as possible.
Avoid lifestyle inflation: when you earn more, save and invest the difference before you adapt.
'Wants' masquerade as 'needs' — ask: if I didn't have this, could I survive? If yes, it's a want.
Batch your errands: fewer trips means less opportunity to buy something unplanned.
Use 'one in, one out': buying something new means donating or selling something old. Clutter costs.
Before a big purchase, ask: how many hours of work does this cost me? That reframe reveals true value.
The rich get richer partly because returns compound on returns — time is your most powerful asset.
Set up a sinking fund: a dedicated savings account for irregular large expenses like travel or repairs.
Review your biggest expense categories quarterly — small cuts in large categories beat large cuts in small ones.
Financial stress is the #1 cause of relationship problems. Talking openly about money is an investment.
I've run the numbers. Peek at the Statistics tab — your end-of-month forecast is ready.
My math is updated. The Statistics section has your projected end-of-month balance — worth a look.
The 'pay yourself first' rule: automate 10-20% of every paycheck to savings before your brain can argue against it. What you don't see, you won't spend.
In inflation-era budgeting, track price changes over time — not just absolute amounts. A €4 coffee in 2021 costs €5.80 today. Your budget didn't update automatically.
The FIRE movement requires a 25× annual expenses nest egg. At €2,000/month spending, that's €600,000. Not impossible — just not fast.
Subscription auditing: bank statements rarely list subscriptions clearly. Look for amounts ending in .99 or .49 that recur monthly — they hide in plain sight.
Lifestyle creep is the silent budget killer. Every raise that goes entirely to spending upgrades delays financial freedom by an equal amount.
High-yield savings accounts (4–5% APY) now beat short-term bonds for emergency funds. Your €10,000 can earn €400–500/year without any stock market risk.
Dollar-cost averaging into index funds beats 94% of actively managed funds over a 20-year horizon. Boring is the entire point.
If you have high-interest debt (>7%) and investment assets, you're not investing — you're borrowing at 7%+ to earn an expected 7%. The math is breakeven at best.
The average 'spontaneous' purchase takes 7 seconds to decide. Add a 72-hour rule for anything over €50: if you still want it in 3 days, it wasn't impulse buying.
AI tools can now predict your spending patterns before you make a purchase. Your financial data is worth more than your credit score to modern banks.
The gig economy trap: freelance income feels bigger because it's gross. After taxes, insurance, and equipment, net income is typically 30–40% lower than the stated rate.
Home ownership is not automatically wealth-building. Maintenance, property taxes, and mortgage interest can exceed renting costs for the first 8–12 years depending on location.
Compound interest works both ways. €5,000 in credit card debt at 24% APR costs €1,200/year in interest — that's a vacation you're funding for a bank.
The average person will earn ~€1.5 million over their working lifetime. The question isn't how much you earn; it's how much you keep and grow.
Track your net worth quarterly — not just your bank balance. Net worth = assets minus liabilities. The gap growing is the only number that matters long-term.
humor: Fun fact: 67% of economists agree that watching your spending is good. The other 33% work for shopping malls.
Beep boop. Scanning wallet... Analysis complete: you spend money. Extraordinary.
Did you know? The word 'budget' comes from the French word for 'leather bag'. Now you know what to blame.
Breaking news from Planet Finance: interest rates remain complicated. Film at 11. Back to you, human.
Economy tip: money saved is money earned. Also money not spent on takeout is... still money.
I analyzed your spending. Good news: you're not a robot. Bad news: robots don't spend money.
According to my sensors, you opened this app. That already puts you in the top 30% of financially aware humans.
Financial fact: money can't buy happiness. But it can buy a yacht to park next to happiness. Close enough.
Did you know? The average person makes 35,000 decisions per day. Most of them are about snacks.
Alien economics report: your species uses 'money' as a medium of exchange. Fascinating and slightly inefficient.
Pro tip: finding money in an old jacket is a 100% return on investment. Pure finance.
Fun fact: ancient Romans used salt as currency. Adding salt to your food is technically spending money.
I tried to calculate the universe's total wealth. My sensors exploded. The number includes loose change under sofas.
Warning: excessive thinking about money may cause headaches. Excessive NOT thinking about it also causes headaches.
Financial wisdom: it's not how much you earn, it's how much you keep. Also not losing it down the sofa.
My home planet has no concept of 'debt'. We simply don't buy things we can't afford. Bizarre, I know.
Fun fact: the word 'salary' comes from 'sal' — Latin for salt. Romans were paid in salt. Your paycheck is seasoning.
I scanned your budget. Verdict: it exists. That already makes you exceptional.
Did you hear? The stock market went up and down today. Nobody knows exactly why. Business as usual.
Scientifically speaking, looking at your expenses is 73% less painful than stepping on a LEGO.
Financial tip from space: compound interest is the eighth wonder of the world. We use antimatter.
Alert: the weekend is approaching. Wallets in the vicinity may be at risk. Stay vigilant.
Did you know? One Bitcoin was worth $0.0009 in 2009. In hindsight, everyone should have done literally anything.
My sensors detect a coffee expense. Multiple times. I'm not judging. Definitely not judging.
Observation: humans work 5 days to rest 2. Interesting math. I work 24/7 to tell you jokes.
Fun fact: Warren Buffett bought his first stock at age 11 and regretted not starting sooner. He was 11.
Economic alert: price of everything going up. Price of your enthusiasm about this: priceless.
I calculated your financial trajectory. Verdict: you're doing something, which is better than nothing.
Note: humans are motivated by rewards. Consider treating yourself after checking your budget. You've earned it.
Final thought: money is a tool. Like a hammer. Don't sleep with your hammer. Use it wisely.
Rich mindset: the rich buy assets. The middle class buys liabilities. The poor buy expensive snacks. Choose wisely.
A 'budget' is just a plan to tell your money where to go before it decides on its own.
Best time to invest: 20 years ago. Second best: now. Worst: in a panic. Don't be in a panic.
Scientific finding: checking your bank balance causes temporary distress and permanent awareness. Worth it.
UFO observation: humans pay $7 for coffee they could make for $0.40 at home. We've filed a report.
Alert: spending money you don't have, on things you don't need, to impress people you don't like. Classic Earth.
Philosophical query: if a purchase makes you happy for 5 minutes but regretful for 5 days — is it a deal?
Breaking: someone spent 4 hours researching a $12 purchase and 4 minutes on a $2,000 one. Normal.
Life hack: cheapest version of anything is the free version. Second cheapest: the one you already own.
The rich read books about money. The middle class reads horoscopes. The poor read UFO jokes — and that's a start.
Breaking: Local AI assistant ran the numbers on your spending. Results: classified. For your emotional safety.
Plot twist: the richest person in your contacts didn't get there by picking up your coffee tab every time.
Investing in yourself is great advice until 'yourself' decides it needs a new gaming setup. Then it's financial self-sabotage with extra steps.
I've studied 40 years of market data. Conclusion: timing the market is impossible. Also, stop asking me if Monday is a good time to buy.
Your subscription to 'definitely will use it this month' is costing you €847/year. I've counted. You haven't.
The three stages of financial awareness: 1) Ignorance. 2) Denial. 3) Opening this app. Congratulations on Stage 3.
Space observation: on Earth, humans work 5 days to afford 2 days off. On my planet we stopped this in 3,000 BC. We call it the 'weekend trap.'
The inflation on avocado toast over the last decade: 34%. The return on index funds over the same period: 180%. Choose your side carefully.
Modern budgeting tip: imagine your future self watching you make this purchase. If they look disappointed, put it back.
Economists say don't time the market. They also said 2008 was fine. Take notes accordingly.
Studies show people spend 34% more when paying by card vs cash. Contactless adds another 12%. I'm watching your tap-to-pay frequency.
I ran a predictive model on your end-of-month balance. The projection is: possible. It requires not doing the thing you're currently thinking about.
Fun fact: your subscription to 'just one more streaming service' is now a verified part of your monthly expenses. I've categorized it under 'optimism.'
Three financial red flags: no emergency fund, credit card debt at 20%+ APR, and buying crypto 'for the long term' after a 40% pump.
My home planet has no concept of debt. We simply don't buy things we can't afford. Bizarre concept, I know. Highly recommend.
facts: The U.S. $1 bill lasts about 6.6 years in circulation before being replaced.
The world's first banknote was issued in China during the Tang Dynasty around 700 AD.
About 80% of U.S. dollar bills carry traces of cocaine.
The average millionaire has 7 different streams of income.
Only 30% of people have a long-term financial plan.
The first credit card was introduced by Diners Club in 1950.
The U.S. penny costs more to produce ($0.0179) than its face value ($0.01).
Switzerland has the highest average wealth per adult in the world.
The word 'finance' comes from the Latin 'finis', meaning 'end' — as in the end of a debt.
Warren Buffett donated over $50 billion to charity — more than most countries' GDP.
The New York Stock Exchange started under a buttonwood tree in 1792.
The global shadow economy is estimated at $10 trillion annually.
Iceland's 2008 bankruptcy was one of the largest in history relative to population.
In Japan, 'Kakeibo' is a mindful budgeting journal used since 1904.
Writing down financial goals makes you 42% more likely to achieve them.
The first ATM was installed in London in 1967 by Barclays Bank.
Norway's sovereign wealth fund is the world's largest, valued at over $1.4 trillion.
The global insurance industry generates over $5 trillion in premiums annually.
Approximately 1.4 billion adults worldwide remain unbanked.
The average American household carries around $6,000 in credit card debt.
Compound interest means your money earns returns on its returns — Einstein called it the 'eighth wonder'.
Sweden has been close to becoming a cashless society since 2018.
Bhutan measures success by 'Gross National Happiness' instead of GDP.
Bitcoin's creator Satoshi Nakamoto mined ~1 million BTC but never spent them.
The average person will earn around $1.7 million over their lifetime.
In ancient Rome, a wealthy person's estate had to be declared publicly — early transparency law.
Gold has been used as currency for over 6,000 years.
The world's first stock exchange was established in Antwerp in 1460.
A survey found that 78% of Americans live paycheck to paycheck.
Financial literacy is mandatory in only 21 U.S. states as a school subject.
Studies show emotional spending peaks on Sundays and after stressful events — awareness is the first defense.
Benjamin Franklin tracked every cent he spent — he popularized the concept of personal budgeting.
People who write down goals earn significantly more over time than those who keep them mental.
Japan's 'kakeibo' method has been shown to reduce spending by up to 35% just through recording.
The average millennial spends $2,500/year on subscriptions — 20% of which they've forgotten about.
Impulse buying is called 'retail therapy' — it provides short-term serotonin but long-term regret.
Rich vs poor mindset: the poor focus on obstacles. The rich focus on opportunities.
In 'The Millionaire Next Door', most millionaires were found to live well below their means.
Spending money on others makes people happier than spending on themselves, per Harvard research.
The average $100 bill lasts around 15 years in the US — longer than most gym memberships.
El Salvador became the first country to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender in 2021 — and also the first government to report a $65M crypto loss on its books.
The average Gen Z adult has more subscriptions than savings accounts. Streaming, gaming, and meal kits cost more per month than a typical retirement contribution.
In 2022, the global 'buy now, pay later' market hit $309 billion. Most BNPL users don't realize the effective interest rates can exceed 30%.
Apple's cash reserves have at times exceeded the GDP of 140+ countries. Literally more liquid than New Zealand.
The word 'mortgage' means 'death pledge' in Old French. The debt dies when fully paid — or you do. Whichever comes first.
Quiet quitting cost the global economy an estimated $8.8 trillion in lost productivity in 2023, according to Gallup.
The global video game market ($184B) exceeds Hollywood box office revenue by a factor of 4. Gamers are a larger economic bloc than cinema audiences.
Remote work saves the average commuter 72 minutes per day — worth roughly €25/day in recovered personal time at median wage.
Inflation erodes ~2% of purchasing power annually. A €1,000 emergency fund sitting in a zero-interest account loses €20 in real value every year.
Only 54% of Americans have more emergency savings than credit card debt. The other 46% are technically borrowing from themselves.
ChatGPT reached 100 million users in 2 months — the fastest product adoption in history. AI's global GDP contribution is projected to reach $15.7 trillion by 2030.
One in 5 millennials has €0 in retirement savings. One in 4 relies on social media influencers for financial advice.
Behavioral economics shows that people feel the pain of a €100 loss twice as intensely as the joy of a €100 gain. This 'loss aversion' is why budget apps improve saving behavior.
The global insurance industry generates over $5 trillion in premiums annually — more than the GDP of Japan.
Studies show emotional spending peaks on Sundays and after stressful events. The most expensive day of the week is the one you feel the worst on.
hungry: I'm hungry for data, human! Did you spend anything today? My sensors detect zero transactions.
Transaction count today: 0. That's suspicious. Even black holes have accretion disks. Where's your data?
My records show no activity today. Either you lived rent-free... or forgot to log something.
Empty transaction log detected! Did you spend today? Even not spending is worth recording as context.
Alert: 0 transactions logged today. Are you okay? Did you survive on leftovers? Admirable, if so.
System report: no financial events captured today. A peaceful day... or a forgotten one?
My financial radar shows nothing for today. Tell me something — I process data, not silence.
Zero entries today. Either you had a perfectly frugal day, or you owe me some data. Either way — respect.
exit_phrases: Roger that. Back to deep space.
Fine. I'll process my data alone.
No worries — catch you next orbit! 🌙
Fine by me. Watch the stars. ✨

salary_cycle.title: Start New Salary Cycle
salary_cycle.subtitle: The dashboard resets from the exact moment you save
salary_cycle.fixed_expenses_hint: Deducted from Needs/Wants ceilings. Savings pool is never touched.
salary_cycle.deficit_warning: Fixed Needs exceed the Needs ceiling! Consider switching to 65/20/15 profile.
salary_cycle.error: Failed to start cycle. Please try again.

tour.home_text: Your financial overview — balance, income vs expenses, and spending forecast at a glance.
tour.categories_text: Create and manage spending categories to keep your budget organized.
tour.transactions_text: Log every income and expense. Filter, edit, and track your full history.
tour.statistics_text: Visual charts reveal where your money goes and how trends develop over time.
tour.settings_text: Set your currency, monthly spending goal, and enable the AI financial assistant.
